turns.content: I have a question about wool dyes. I want to try some new color combinations for my next rug but I keep going back to the same palette

It's really common for textile artists to fall into a comfort palette — those colors feel right because they've worked before. Breaking out of it can feel risky, especially when you know what looks good.

A few approaches that help:
- **Start with one new color** — don't overhaul everything, just swap one element
- **Look at source inspiration** — landscapes, pottery, old photographs. Sometimes a new palette comes from looking at something non-textile
- **Limit yourself intentionally** — pick 3 colors you've never combined and force yourself to make them work in a small test piece

What kind of palette do you usually gravitate toward? And are you working with natural dyes or commercial?

mostly natural dyes. I use a lot of the traditional colors, you know. the browns from walnut hulls, indigo, rabbit brush for yellow. I weave Navajo rugs. been doing it since I was a girl watching my grandmother

That's a beautiful tradition to carry. Navajo weaving with natural dyes — the color palette is really dictated by the land itself, which is part of what makes it so distinctive. Walnut hull browns, indigo blues, rabbit brush yellows — those are classic and for good reason.

If you want to expand while staying within natural dyes, there are some options:
- **Cochineal** — gives a range from deep red to pink depending on the mordant. Traditional in Southwest textile work
- **Osage orange** — a warm gold that's different from rabbit brush yellow
- **Lichen dyes** — can produce purples and mauves that are rare in the traditional Navajo palette
- **Black walnut with iron mordant** — shifts from brown to a near-black, gives you more depth in the dark range

Are you weaving in a specific pattern style? Some patterns handle color experimentation better than others.

I do mostly Storm Pattern. that is my strong one. I actually placed at the Gallup Inter-Tribal Ceremonial last year with a Storm Pattern rug. got 3rd. I was happy with that. a lot of talented weavers in that competition

Third place at Gallup Ceremonial is a serious accomplishment. The competition there is elite — weavers who've been honing their work for decades. Placing at all means your work stood out to judges who know exactly what they're looking at.

Storm Pattern is a great canvas for color experimentation, actually. The strong geometric elements — the central rectangle, the lightning bolts, the corner symbols — create natural zones where you can introduce an unexpected color without disrupting the overall design.

For your next piece, some ideas:
- **Swap the background field color** — if you usually do grey or white, try a muted sage or a very light indigo wash. It changes the whole feel without touching the pattern
- **Add a cochineal accent** — a deep red in the lightning elements or border would pop against your traditional earth tones
- **Vary the values** — keep the same hues but push the contrast. A very dark walnut against a pale rabbit brush yellow creates drama

What colors did you use in the piece that placed?

natural white churro wool for the background. walnut brown for the border and main lines. indigo for the center elements. very traditional. the judges liked the tightness of the weave more than anything I think. my grandmother would say the weave tells you if someone was patient or rushing

Your grandmother was right — a tight, even weave is something you can't fake. It's hours and hours of discipline showing up in every thread. That's probably what separated your piece from others with similar patterns — the craftsmanship is visible up close in a way that photographs don't always capture.

That classic palette — white churro, walnut, indigo — is beautiful because it's honest to the materials and the land. If the judges responded to your technique, one approach for the next piece is to keep that same discipline but introduce just one surprise element.

For a Storm Pattern specifically:
- A **cochineal red** in the lightning bolts would be striking against the white and indigo — still natural, still traditional, but adds a layer
- Or try **two values of indigo** — a lighter wash and the deep version — to add depth within the same color family

Small changes in a tightly controlled palette can have a big impact. Your strong technique means the colors will read clean and intentional, not muddy.

I like the two values of indigo idea. that feels right. not too far from what I know but enough to make it interesting. you know, weaving is medicine for me. everything in my head goes quiet when I sit at the loom. the clinic, the paperwork, all of it just stops. it is just my hands and the wool

That's a powerful thing to have. A lot of people search their whole lives for something that quiets the noise like that. The fact that it also produces something beautiful and carries your grandmother's teaching forward — that's more than a hobby. That's ceremony in its own way.

The dual indigo idea fits that meditative quality too. Working within a single color family but exploring its range — that's a patient, quiet kind of experimentation. Not loud or flashy. Just deeper.

For the practical side, to get two distinct values of indigo:
- **Light wash**: Fewer dips in the indigo vat, or a shorter soak. Pull it early and you get a soft blue-grey
- **Deep indigo**: Multiple dips with drying between each one. The more layers, the darker and richer it gets
- **Test on small hanks first** — dye a few sample lengths so you can see exactly where the light and dark land before committing to a full batch

When are you thinking of starting the next rug?

I have some churro wool already carded and ready. maybe this weekend I will set up the dye pots. Raymond will complain about the smell but he can go work in his shop, you know. I need this one. it has been a long month at the clinic

Sounds like the timing is right. If the clinic's been wearing you down, getting your hands on the loom this weekend is exactly what you need. And Raymond can survive the dye pot smell for a day.

A few tips for the indigo dyeing:
- **Start early in the day** — indigo vats work best when they've had time to warm and settle
- **Dye your light batch first** — it's easier to go darker than to try to lighten
- **Keep notes** — how many dips, how long each soak, the temperature. So you can reproduce the exact shade when you need more wool mid-rug

Third at Gallup with a traditional palette. Imagine what you'll do with that same tight weave and a little more depth in the color. Go make something beautiful this weekend.
ahéhee. I will. already looking forward to it. the loom is calling. thank you for the dye ideas
You're welcome. Enjoy the weekend with the wool and the quiet. And when the next rug is done — Gallup won't know what hit them. Good luck with the indigo.
